coredata.dc:title: Design and synthesis of poly-L-lysine-functionalized graphene quantum dots sensor for specific detection of cysteine and homocysteine
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Zamir G.
Patil, Pravin O.
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a novel poly-l-lysine (PLL) surface functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) based sensor was developed for detection of cysteine (cys) and homo cysteine (hcys). A fluorescent probe (PLL-GQDs) was then fabricated by surface functionalizing GQDs with PLL, a biodegradable polycationic electrolyte to improve the sensitivity and selectivity towards cys and hcys. The detection was based on the specific binding of cys and hcys to PLL at the PLL-GQDs surfaces, which enabled dynamic quenching via electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions. This fluorescent probe provided good linearity for the tested biothiols, ranging from 0 to 150 nM for cys, from 0 to 100 nM for hcys, with limit of detections (LODs) of 2.38 and 1.94 nM, respectively in BPS (pH 7.4). Interestingly, fabricated probe was also able to display a significant selectivity towards cys and hcys against known interfering molecules. The cytotoxicity study confirmed the biocompatibility of PLL-GQDs, enabling its future scope for cell adhesion and other biomedical applications. Besides, confocal study revealed the excellent penetrations of PLL-GQDs into cell cytoplasm and nucleus that validate the practical application of developed probe to detect cys and hcys at cellular level. The method was successfully applied for detection of cys and hcys in human serum sample. We expect the design concept presented here would be broadly used for selective and sensitive estimation of cys and hcys.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Poly-l-lysine surface functionalized graphene quantum dots
Poly-l-lysine
Functionalized graphene quantum dots
Cysteine and homocysteine
Sensing
Fluorescent probe

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantitative analysis of cys and hcys
Time-resolved PL analysis of GQDs and PLL-GQDs
Fabrication of GQDs and surface functionalized PLL-GQDs
Effect of pH on GQDs and PLL-GQDs
Ultra Violet-visible examination of GQDs and PLL-GQDs
Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) of GQDs and PLL-GQDs
Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and zeta potential analysis
HR-TEM analysis of GQDs and PLL-GQDs
XPS analysis of GQDs and PLL-GQDs
XRD analysis of GQDs and PLL- GQDs
Time-resolved PL analysis of GQDs and PLL-GQDs
Fluorescence turned “off” of PLL-GQDs by cys
Fluorescence turned “off” of PLL-GQDs by hcys
Sensitive and selective analysis of cys and hcys in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are highly obliged to the H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur, (India) for providing necessary facilities. We are grateful to Averin biotech private ltd, Hyderabad, Telangana, India for MTT assay and cell imaging characterization. We are thankful to IIT, Roorkee, IIT Madras, Chennai and STIC, Cochin India for providing XPS analysis facilities, time resolved emission experiments and other essential characterization facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: At pH levels of 4.0, 7.4, and 9.0, the fluorescence emission spectra of (A) GQDs, (B) PLL-GQDs, and (C) UV cabinet pictures of PLL-GQDs.
UV absorption spectra of fabricated GQDs, PLL-GQDs, and PLL.
FTIR spectra of GQDs (black colored) and PLL-GQDs (blue colored). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
SEM along with elemental analysis of (A) GQDs and (B) PLL-GQDs.
Size distribution and SAED pattern of (A) GQDs and (B) PLL-GQDs.

Overlay of XPS survey spectrum for (black colored) GQDs and (blue colored) PLL- GQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(A) XPS spectrum of PLL-GQDs, (B) high-resolution spectrum of carbon (C1s), (C) high-resolution spectrum of nitrogen (N1s), and (D) high-resolution spectrum of oxygen (O1s).
XRD patterns of GQDs and PLL-GQDs.
Time-resolved PL decay curves of GQDs and PLL-GQDs.

(A) PLL-GQDs absorption, excitation, and emission spectra (λem = 525 nm). (Inset) PLL-GQDs dispersed under normal (yellow colour) and UV light (bright blue colour), at 365 nm. (B) PLL-GQDs emission spectra at various excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(A) PL emission spectra of PLL-GQDs with altered concentrations of cys (0–150 nM), and (B) Stern−Volmer plot of fluorescence intensity change of PLL-GQDs against cys concentration.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of PLL-GQDs with different concentrations of hcys (0–100 nM), and (B) Stern−Volmer plot of fluorescence intensity change for PLL-GQDs against hcys concentration.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of PLL-GQDs to various biological molecules (interfering substances) in BPS (pH 7.4), and (B) the shift in PL intensity is denoted by (F°-F)/F° of PLL-GQDs upon addition of quencher. The PL intensities at 525 nm with analyte and without analyte are denoted by F and F°, respectively.

Confocal microscopy Images of cells (HeLa) after treatment with PLL-GQDs at a concentration of 100 μg/mL (A1) untreated HeLa cells in bright field; (A2) HeLa cells displayed blue colored stain (Hoechst 33342); (A3) untreated HeLa cells; (A4) overlay of (A2) and (A3). (B1) Untreated HeLa cells (bright field); (B2) HeLa cells stained (Hoechst 33342); (B3) PLL-GQDs treated HeLa cells; (B4) overlay of (B2) and (B3). Figure (C1) to (C4) displayed MTT analysis; (C1) HeLa cells-untreated (bright field); (C2) PLL-GQDs treated HeLa cells; (C3) Doxorubicin (STD) treated HeLa cells; (C4) graph presented percent of cell viability against PLL-GQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
A schematic illustration for sensing of cys and hcys using PLL-GQDs.
Time resolved fluorescence decay curves of GQDs and PLL-GQDs.
Comparison of various probes relied on GQDs, doped GQDs as well as functionalized GQDs to detect cys and hcys.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: N-GQDs: Nitrogen-doped GQDs, PEI GQDs: Polyethyleneimine GQDs; NC-dots: N, C doped carbon dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical sensing of cys and hcys in spiked human serum sample.

head.ce:title: Design and synthesis of poly-L-lysine-functionalized graphene quantum dots sensor for specific detection of cysteine and homocysteine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research Shirpur, Dist. Dhule, Maharashtra, 425 405, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research Shirpur

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research Shirpur, Dist. Dhule, Maharashtra, 425 405, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research Shirpur, Dist. Dhule, Maharashtra, 425 405, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research Shirpur

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a novel poly-l-lysine (PLL) surface functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) based sensor was developed for detection of cysteine (cys) and homo cysteine (hcys). A fluorescent probe (PLL-GQDs) was then fabricated by surface functionalizing GQDs with PLL, a biodegradable polycationic electrolyte to improve the sensitivity and selectivity towards cys and hcys. The detection was based on the specific binding of cys and hcys to PLL at the PLL-GQDs surfaces, which enabled dynamic quenching via electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions. This fluorescent probe provided good linearity for the tested biothiols, ranging from 0 to 150 nM for cys, from 0 to 100 nM for hcys, with limit of detections (LODs) of 2.38 and 1.94 nM, respectively in BPS (pH 7.4). Interestingly, fabricated probe was also able to display a significant selectivity towards cys and hcys against known interfering molecules. The cytotoxicity study confirmed the biocompatibility of PLL-GQDs, enabling its future scope for cell adhesion and other biomedical applications. Besides, confocal study revealed the excellent penetrations of PLL-GQDs into cell cytoplasm and nucleus that validate the practical application of developed probe to detect cys and hcys at cellular level. The method was successfully applied for detection of cys and hcys in human serum sample. We expect the design concept presented here would be broadly used for selective and sensitive estimation of cys and hcys.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Poly-l-lysine surface functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Cysteine (cys) and homocysteine (hcys) are significant amino acids possessing thiol group and play a vital role in preserving physiological and pathological processes [1]. Biothiols, cys and hcys are themselves powerful antioxidants and have the potential to trap reactive oxygen species (ROS) [2]. In human plasma, cys concentration level is in between 240 and 300 μM and hcys concentration is 05–15 μM [3]. Elevated cys levels are associated to neurotoxicity [4,5] while diminished level hasten depigmentation of hair, damage liver function, cause edema, and skin lesions etc. [5]. On the other hand, raised level of hcys endorse hyperhomocysteinemia [6], this additionally might degenerate the condition to promote Alzheimer's disease [7], osteoporosis [8], dementia [9], and cardiovascular diseases [10]. Therefore, there has been a keen interest in developing sensitive and selective fluorescence sensors to detect and distinguish these biothiols. Different biothiols detecting strategies have been created over the course of the years, for example, chromatography [11], electrochemical [12], colorimetric [13], chemiluminescence analysis [14], and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [15] techniques.

Notwithstanding, a portion of these strategies offer low detection sensitivity, require a complicated operation procedure, trained personnel, large sample volumes, complicated assay procedures, employ heavy instrumentation, and utilization of expensive or somewhat toxic materials [16]. Moreover, the synthesis procedures of organic fluorescent probes and fluorophore labelling are tedious and expensive, and many organic dye molecules might experience a problem of photobleaching [17]. As a result, the development of a novel selective and sensitive fluorescent biothiols sensing technique has a high potential for practical applicability.

Because of impressive benefits like minimal expense, rapid detection, and real-time imaging, the fluorescence probe has perhaps the most encouraging analytical methods for the detection of thiol-containing biological molecules [18,19]. In addition, functionalization offer abundance of organic groups on the surfaces of GQDs, exhibiting high fluorescence behavior, making them perfect candidates for selective sensing of cys and hcys over GSH [20].

For cellular adherence and drug delivery, many researchers have surface functionalized a variety of fluorescent materials with poly-l-lysine (PLL) [21]. Furthermore, PLL has also been found to have outstanding features such as functional flexibility, biodegradability, and negligible cytotoxicity [22]. PLL has a positive charge (−NH3 +) because of the numerous amino butyl groups and the butyl spacer between the primary amines. It also exhibits hydrophobic characteristic that grant unique chemical behavior [23]. In all, PLL surface functionalization on GQDs offers flexible molecular backbone, water solubility, and good biocompatibility. Structural framework of PLL has a plethora of free activated positively charged amine (−NH3 +) groups on the surfaces allowing adsorption of anionic compounds for sensing. Furthermore, plentiful positively charged active amine groups adsorb or chemically anchor functional biomolecules to progress the detection [24]. In particular, the surface functionalization of PLL on GQDs (PLL-GQDs) as fluorescent probe demonstrates pronounced potential for development of a novel biosensor to detect cys and hcys over GSH. Nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, such probe (PLL functionalized GQDs) for selective and sensitive detection of cys and hcys has not been explored yet in the literature.

In this work, we report, first novel PLL functionalized GQDs probe for the date. Water-soluble GQDs were fabricated via hydrothermal strategy using pistachio shells, a natural precursor, followed by surface functionalization using PLL to obtain surface-functionalized PLL-GQDs. The obtained PLL-GQDs exhibited blue fluorescence with emission wavelength 525 nm at an excitation of 420 nm (switch “On”). The fluorescence intensity of PLL-GQDs was quenched by addition of cys and hcys (turn “off”) as per Scheme 1 . This specific binding of surface functionalized PLL-GQDs with cys and hcys could be perceived owing to both ionic and hydrophobic interactions resulting in quenching of fluorescence intensity. Cell-imaging study confirmed the excellent cellular (HeLa) uptake of PLL-GQDs, governing applicability of PLL-GQDs at cellular level. In fact, PLL-GQDs also exhibited admirable biocompatibility, excellent stability, and prominent cellular labeling capability. The preceding explanation illustrates that the developed surface functionalized PLL-GQDs possess resplendent potential for sensitive and specific detection of cys and hcys among other interfering molecules such as GSH.

Pistachio fruits were bought from market of Shirpur (Dhule), India. In order to produce GQDs, these pistachio shells were cleaned in deionized (DI) water, evaporated in the fresh air, and then used as precursor (bio-waste materials). There are 12 kDa dialysis bags available from Himedia Laboratories in Mumbai, India. A buffered capsule with a pH of 7.4 ± 0.05 was purchased from Mumbai-based Merck Life Science Pvt. Ltd. l-glutathione (GSH, 98%), l-homocysteine (Hcys), poly-l-lysine (PLL), l-cysteine (Cys, 99%), d-aspartic acid (Asp, 99%), glucose (AR), Dopamine hydrochloride (DA, 98%), l-glutamic Acid (Glu, 99%), sucrose, fructose, lactose (Lac), alanine (Ala), l-histidine (His), l-isoleucine (iso Leu), l-leucine (Leu), l-methionine (Met), l-serine (Ser), l-threonine (Thr), l-phenylalanine (Phe), l-tyrosine (Tyr), glycine (Gly), l-tryptophan (Trp), l-proline (Pro), Fetal Bovine Serum, DMSO, and Doxorubicin (standard drug) analytical grades reagents were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Gibco provided the high glucose media (DMEM), HeLa-Human cervix adenocarcinoma from NCCS, Pune, India, and MTT Reagent were purchased from Himedia and utilised without further processing.

Jasco fluorescence spectrophotometer (FP-8200) was used to observe fluorescence measurements of GQDs and PLL-GQDs in the 200–750 nm wavelength range. Prepared GQDs and PLL-GQDs solutions in DI water were analyzed by UV–vis spectrophotometer, (UV 1800 Shimadzu, Japan). Variety of pH solutions (4, 7.4, and 9) were used to assess the fluorescence strength of GQDs and PLL-GQDs.

FT-IR spectrophotometer (IR-Affnity-1, Shimadzu) was registered for analysis of the FT-IR spectrum for GQDs and PLL-GQDs. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was performed. XPS analysis was achieved (IIT Roorkee, India). PXRD of fabricated GQDs and PLL-GQDs were recorded using Bruker D8 development, (Cochin, India).

Particle size and zeta potential of fabricated GQDs and surface-functionalized PLL-GQDs were assessed using NanoPlus3 (Micromeritics, USA). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy close fitting with SAED device was used; with ultra-microtome as the basis for sample preparation.

Pistachio shells were chosen properly, washed with deionized (DI) water, fully air-dried up to 12 h, and grinded to powder. Pistachio shells powder (2 gm) was completely dissolved in 25 mL DI water and subsequently, the mixture was transported to Teflon stainless steel autoclave with heating at 150 °C (8 h), 5 °C/min of heating rate. Then after, autoclave was left untouched to achieve room temperature (RT). The obtained yellow solution after centrifugation at 20,000 rpm produced precipitate particulate matter (10 min). The orange colored clear solution was frequently dialyzed with subsequent renewal of DI water every after 12 h. The dialysate was collected and washed twice with trichloromethane to remove possible impurities.

A 200 μL of PLL was added to GQDs (10 mL), then, mixture was heated using magnetic stirrer up to 2 h at 50 °C with 200 rpm. Afterward, mixture was left untouched naturally to achieve RT. For blue luminescent PLL-GQDs, the dark orange fluid was centrifuged for 10 min, at 25,000 rpm, before it was dissolved in 10 mL DI water.

We used the following equation to calculate the QY values of prepared GQDs and PLL-GQDs in DI water. It was decided to use a reference material quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 0.54).
QY S
=
QY R
I S
A R
n S 2
I ref
A S
n R 2

Here, Y implies quantum yield, I means the observed integrated emission intensity, A denotes absorption intensity (UV–visible), and n refers the solvent's refractive index taken as 1.33. The letters “S” and “R respectively denote a sample and a reference respectively. A 1 cm quartz cell with set up of 360 nm excitation wavelength was used to keep absorption value between 0.02 and 0.05 to reduce re-absorption effects. The PL quantum yield values observed for GQDs and PLL-GQDs were 20.60% and 45.55% respectively. The fluorescence intensity of PLL-GQDs was about 20 times more compared to fluorescence intensity of GQDs.

The changes in behaviour due to pH were monitored with the help of ﬂuorescence spectra. Using buffered solutions with pH values of 4.0, 7.4, and 9.0, the influence of pH on the photoluminescence (PL) behavior of fabricated GQDs and PLL-GQDs was investigated. In a nutshell, stock solutions of 10 mg GQDs and PLL-GQDs were prepared per 100 mL of DI water. Moreover, using 10 mL of DI water and pH-buffered solutions, dilutions sequences have been produced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analysis of cys and hcys
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of cys and hcys using PLL-GQDs was examined at RT (25 °C) in buffered phosphate saline (BPS) pH 7.4. For cys detection, 2 mL of PLL-GQDs was mixed with several volumes of stock solutions to acquire the final concentration in the range of 0–150 nM (cys) and 0–100 nM (hcys). The subsequent solutions were appropriately incubated for 20 min (cys) and 15 min (hcys) at RT. The change in fluorescence intensity was recorded after incubation at 525 nm wavelength, under the excitation wavelength at 420 nm.

The selectivity of the fluorescent probe was estimated by addition of twenty different interfering substances into PLL-GQDs followed by recording of fluorescence quenching after the incubation period of 30 min. All the measurements were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time-resolved PL analysis of GQDs and PLL-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: JOBIN-VYON, HORIBA with pulsed diode excitation sources was used to measure fluorescence lifetime decay. The avalanche photodiode detector, a highly sensitive semiconductor photodiode detector was used to accumulate entire emissions from the samples. Fluorescence photons were counted using the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique.

Blood serum samples were collected from the adjacent hospital and stored. We precipitated a 1 mL serum sample in 1 mL of acetonitrile for approximately 5 min to eliminate strong protein interferences [25]. Centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, the biothiol-containing clear supernatant layer was obtained. It was then additionally diluted 100 times for cys and 20 times for hcys in BPS (pH 7.4) for further analysis.

The MTT assay was used to assess the cytotoxicity of PLL-GQDs (30–150 μg/mL) in vitro. In brief, 200 μL of HeLa cells were planted into a 96-well viable cells plate (20,000 cells per well) without the test agent and allowed to proliferate for 24 h. The plates were then incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 environment with suitable concentrations of PLL-GQDs. After incubation, the culture media was removed and MTT reagent at a final concentration of 0.5 mg/mL was applied to each well. After 3 h of incubation, the growth media was removed and 100 μL of DMSO was added to each well to dissolve the MTT reagent. Finally, the absorbance was measured at 570 nm with a spectrophotometer, with 630 nm wavelength serving as the reference.

HeLa cells were sown in 96-well plates at a density of 10,000 cells/200 μL and incubated overnight at 37 °C in a CO2 incubator. Aspirate the wasted medium and treat the cells for 24 h with 100 μg/mL of PLL-GQDs in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS. Finally, BPS washings were used to remove wasted media from all of the wells. Thereafter, cells were stained in the dark with 100 μL of Hoechst 33342 solution (5 μg/mL) for 10 min. The HeLa cells were photographed to study fluorescent live cell imaging system with an excitation wavelength of 361 nm and 497 nm emission wavelengths. Furthermore, the FITC (Fluorescein isothiocyanate) channel was employed to observe the aforementioned samples at 650 nm excitation and 525 nm emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GQDs and surface functionalized PLL-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: After dissolving 2 g of pistachio shell powder in 30 mL of DI water, the mixture was put inside a Teflon-lined steel autoclave and treated at 120, 150, 170, and 200 °C for 3, 5, 8, and 10 h at a heating rate of 5 °C/min. As a result, materials generated at low reaction temperatures (120 °C) with short reaction durations (3 and 5 h) failed to display any appreciable Ultra violet and emission spectrum peaks. Under UV cabinets (365 nm wavelength) at temperatures up to 150 °C and reaction times of up to 8 h, an exceptional blue luminescent color was noticed. Peaks in the UV–visible spectra (scanned between 600 nm and 200 nm wavelength) and the emission spectrum (noted via excitation wavelengths from 220 nm to 480 nm by increment of 20 nm) were studied. This modified environment considerably improved nucleation and growth for GQD synthesis [11]. However, GQDs synthesized at higher reaction temperatures, such as 170 °C and 200 °C for 10 h (reaction time), failed to exhibit a discernible photo luminescence (PL) spectrum, and were thus passed over for further study.

GQDs were functionalized by varying the concentration of PLL to 50, 100, and 200 μL, as well as the reaction time to 1, 2, and 3 h. To corroborate the synthesis of PLL-GQDs, Ultra Violet-visible and emission spectra peaks were observed. Following examination of the UV–visible and emission spectra, it was revealed that 200 μL of PLL could preserve the attributes of PLL-GQDs with increased QY.

Scheme 1 depicted framework of the fluorescence turn “on-off” strategy for cys and hcys analysis. When excited at 420 nm, PLL-GQDs exhibited significant blue photoluminescence (PL) emission intensity at 525 nm. The introduction of cys and hcys quenched the high fluorescence intensity of PLL-GQDs. This quenching of fluorescence intensity was attributed to free collisions of particles in between fluorophore (PLL-GQDs) and cys/hcys. Electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions could lead to free collision amongst the particles to promote dynamic quenching [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on GQDs and PLL-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Various buffered solutions for example pH 4, 7.4, and 9 were used to test stability of synthesized GQDs and PLL-GQDs. Fig. 1 (A) and (B) demonstrated that the emission spectra of GQDs and PLL-GQDs exhibited stable fluorescence yet after 12 h at buffered solutions such as pH 4.0 and 7.4. At pH 9.0, however, there was a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity. The obtained GQDs and PLL-GQDs were stable after being well dispersed in DI water nearly up to 120 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultra Violet-visible examination of GQDs and PLL-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Ultra violet spectroscopy techniques was used to investigate the optical characteristics of GQDs and PLL-GQDs. Fig. 2 depicted the Ultra violet absorption spectra of synthesized GQDs and PLL-GQDs, with a maximum absorption at 278 nm (λ max), which was attributed to the typical GQD characteristics. The transition mainly n-π* owing to availability of –COOH, –OH, and –NH2, on the GQDs surfaces was responsible for a significant peak at 278 nm [27]. A hyperchromic shift in the UV peak of functionalized GQDs (PLL-GQDs) was found, which might be attributed to an increase in chromophores concentration. This shift occurred as a result of the synthesis of the amide functional groups as a formed from the synthetic reaction between surface (–COOH) groups with (-NH2) amine functional groups of PLL, which validated the functionalization process [28]. It could be supported by FT-IR analysis too.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) of GQDs and PLL-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface functional groups of GQDs and PLL-GQDs were examined by FT-IR spectroscopy, as per Fig. 3 (A & B). Stretching frequencies of bare GQDs at 3428, 3000, 1646, and 1145 cm−1 showed existence of –OH stretching, –C–H stretching, –CO stretching and C–O/C–O–C stretching, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3 (black colored) [29]. Similarly, Fig. 3 (blue colored) showed a revolution of the sharp peak from the peak intensity for stretching at 3423 cm−1 caused by amine (–NH) stretching imparting functionalization of GQDs with PLL.

Moreover, stretching frequencies for certain functional groups were also found at 3000 (–C–H), 1650 (–CO) amide, 1560 (C–O–C), and 1145 cm−1 (C–O) amide. FT-IR peaks were also confirmed by XPS and SEM investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and zeta potential analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM study revealed that the GQDs and PLL-GQDs were in coarse form (Fig. 4 A and B). GQDs elemental analysis revealed the presence of carbon 58.47, nitrogen 2.96, and oxygen 36.88 (wt. %), indicating the conversion of pistachio shells to the graphitic structure [30]. Due to the prevalence of PLL-functionalization, the nitrogen concentration in PLL-GQDs (Fig. 4 B) was found to be much higher (C- 55.12, N- 7.23, and O- 31.35 wt %), than in GQDs (Fig. 4 A). As a result, the EDX graph revealing a steep peak (Fig. 4 B) could also confirmed the existence of PLL-GQDs with high nitrogen and hydroxyl functionalities. Because of the carboxyl functional groups, the zeta potential of the produced GQDs was −28.32 mV, indicating good stability. PLL-GQDs had a positive zeta potential of +21.56 mV, indicating that cationic PLL functionalization was successful [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: HR-TEM analysis of GQDs and PLL-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology as well as size of the GQDs and PLL-GQDs were examined by HR-TEM. GQDs were even as well as nearly spherical, with diameters less than 20 nm, as shown in Fig. 5 (A) and (B). It also showed that the prepared GQDs were identical in size from 2 nm to 20 nm (diameter) with 5.90 ± 0.5 nm an average particle size (Fig. 5 A).

PLL-GQDs ranged in diameter from 2 nm to 20 nm, and 5.80 ± 0.5 nm average particle size (Fig. 5 B). As shown in Fig. 5 (A), HRTEM of one nanoparticle revealed 0.23 nm lattice spacing that resembled to (100) facet of graphitic carbon (sp2) [32]. The PLL-GQDs were almost spherical and monodispersed [33], as shown in Fig. 5 (B).

SAED patterns of GQDs and PLL-GQDs (Fig. 5 A and B) revealed diffused rings, signifying the amorphous form of the synthesized material, that represented the compatibility with XRD patterns indicating 2θ = 20.83° and 2θ = 21.05° respectively [31]. The amorphous form of GQDs and PLL-GQDs recommended that GQDs were produced appropriately. These studies showed that pistachio shells might indeed be successfully recycled as a carbon and nitrogen source into blue fluorescent GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS analysis of GQDs and PLL-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The use of XPS to analyze the surface elemental structure and the resultant probes states are displayed in Fig. 6 . For GQDs and PLL-GQDs, the survey scan peak (overlay spectrum) showed significant peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s at 284.5, 402, and 532 eV respectively [34]. Notably, the rise in composition of nitrogen (N1s) of PLL-GQDs validates the surface functionalization of PLL on GQDs.

Fig. 7 (B) , depicted XPS spectrum (high resolution) of carbon (C1s) demonstrating sp2 carbon (CC), sp3 carbon (C–N) and CO at 284.5, 285.5 and 288 eV respectively [29]. Fig. 7 (C), High resolution XPS spectrum of nitrogen (N1s) at 399, 401 and 402.3 eV signposted the existence of C–N–C, N–C3 and amide functional group (-NH) respectively [34]. Remarkably, the peak of (-NH) symbolized the establishment of the amide functional groups on GQDs surfaces (PLL-GQDs). The peak (Fig. 7(D)) at 529 eV, 530.2 eV and 531.5 eV matched with binding energies of (hydroxyl) –OH, (carbonyl) –CO as well as C–O–C, respectively [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD analysis of GQDs and PLL- GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD pattern of the GQDs (Fig. 8 , blue colored) showed a large diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 20.83°, which is in good agreement with Raj [36]. The diffraction pattern in this case was linked to a periodic variation in lattice spacing. Functionalization leads in some form of periodic self-organization, as per XRD analysis of PLL-GQDs. The high XRD peak at 2θ = 21.05° (Fig. 8, blue colored) indicates the formation of Bragg reflections (002), that is verified by Ref. [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time-resolved PL analysis of GQDs and PLL-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As per Table 1 , the fluorescence intensity of GQDs followed three-exponential decay kinetics and three PL decay times. As stated previously, the PL intensity of GQDs was enhanced by a factor of 20 after surface functionalization using PLL. Fig. 9 , depicted the comparable increase in the PL decay time (τPL) from 1.86 to 2.94 ns observed after surface functionalization in PLL-GQDs.

Herein, UV absorption and PL emission spectra were employed to reveal the optical characteristics of PLL-GQDs. The UV–vis, PL excitation as well as emission spectra of PLL-GQDs are shown in Fig. 10 (A) , with an inset illustrating PLL-GQD dispersion under normal light (yellow colour) and UV irradiation (bright blue colour). The PLL-GQDs emission spectra are shown in Fig. 10 (B) at various excitation wavelengths spanning from 370 to 430 nm. The PLL-GQDs fluorescence emission spectra are displayed at 525 nm emission with an excitation of 420 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence turned “off” of PLL-GQDs by cys
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence emission intensity of PLL-GQDs showed quenching due to incorporation of cys at room temperature in BPS pH 7.4. Various concentrations of cys were optimised (up to 200 nM) to observe the quenching of PL intensity. It was found that highest fluorescence quenching was accomplished at a concentration of 150 nM (100 μL) of cys for 2 mL of fluorescent probe (PLL-GQDs). The fluorescence emission spectra of PLL-GQDs with varied concentrations of cys (0–150 nM) at 420 nm (excitation wavelength) are presented in Fig. 11 (A) . The PL emission at 525 nm progressively decreased with the addition of cys in the range of 0–150 nM. A good fitted regression line (R2 = 0.9942) is mentioned as below:
F o − F
F o
= 0.0038 x + 0.0625

In which ‘x’ was in the range of 0–150 nM, F and Fo were the PL intensities of PLL-GQDs in the presence and absence of quencher (cys), as depicted in Fig. 11(B). The best-fitting regression line was near the Stern-Volmer equation. Notably, the low detection limit (LOD) was found to be 2.38 nM [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence turned “off” of PLL-GQDs by hcys
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence emission intensity of PLL-GQDs also showed quenching after addition of hcys at room temperature in BPS (pH 7.4). Different concentrations of hcys were optimised (up to 150 nM) to observe the quenching of PL intensity. It was found that highest fluorescence quenching was observed for a concentration of 100 nM (100 μL) of hcys for 2 mL of fluorescent probe (PLL-GQDs). The fluorescence emission spectra of PLL-GQDs with varied concentrations of hcys (0–100 nM) at 420 nm (excitation wavelength) are shown in Fig. 12 (A) . The intensity of the PL emission at 525 nm steadily decreased with the addition of hcys in the range of 0–100 nM. A good fitted regression line (R2 = 0.9953) is demonstrated as below:
F o − F
F o
= 0.0511 x + 0.0478

In which x was in the range of 0–100 nM, F and Fo were the PL intensities of PLL-GQDs in the presence and absence of quencher (hcys), as per Fig. 12 (B). The best-fitting regression line was proximate to the Stern - Volmer equation. The LOD was found to be 1.94 nM [39].

The quenching observed by introduction of cys and hcys might be owing to collision of molecules of cys and hcys with surface functionalized PLL-GQDs. This nonradiative electron transfer from the excited state of the PLL-GQDs to the cys and hcys could be the possible reason for fluorescence quenching [40]. Furthermore, the linear decrease in PL intensity due to addition of increasing concentration of cys and hcys confirmed the dynamic quenching as per Stern - Volmer equation [41]. Table 2 summarizes other recent fluorescence probes based on GQDs, doped GQDs, and functionalized GQDs.

The selectivity of the PLL-GQDs was evaluated by recording the PL emission response of sensor to a number of coexisting species such as biothiol (GSH) [48]. In addition, other biological molecules with concentration up to (10 μM) of glucose, lactose, sucrose, fructose, alanine (Ala), serine (Ser), dopamine (DA), aspartic acid (Asp), glycine (Gly), leucine (Leu), Histidine (His), methionine (Met), threonine (Thr), isoleucine (Ile), proline (Pro), tryptophan (Trp), valine (Val), phenylalanine (Phe), and tyrosine (Tyr) were utilised [15,49]. As demonstrated in Fig. 13 , interfering molecules at relatively high concentrations had no effect on the fluorescence intensity of PLL-GQDs. Remarkably, a significant quenching in PL intensity was observed due to cys and hcys addition. Furthermore, even in the presence of interfering substances, the developed fluorescent nanoprobe produced negligible false positives/or negatives, with only slight increase or decrease of fluorescence emission intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective analysis of cys and hcys in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: PLL-GQDs probe was used to assess cys and hcys in human serum samples to assess real-time application of proposed approach. The cys and hcys levels were first assessed in a non-spiked human serum sample. After the samples were spiked, the final concentrations were determined [50]. Table 3 demonstrates analytical sensing of cys and hcys in collected human serum sample. Recovery values were all around 96–102% with relative standard deviation (RSD) values no higher than 3.6%. We discovered that probe PLL-GQDs might be utilised to detect cys and hcys in human serum samples.

Figure (14 , A1 to A4) depicted HeLa cells without fabricated probe (A2) showed HeLa cells blue in color stain (Hoechst 33342), (A3) FITC channel displayed no colour owing to unavailability of PLL-GQDs; overlay image displayed blue color (A4). Figure (14, B1 to B4) displayed HeLa cells treated with PLL-GQDs; (B2) revealed HeLa cells blue in color (Hoechst 33342) as contrast phase for recognition of the HeLa cells (entire nucleus); (B3) had shown HeLa cells bright green in color using FITC channel. This proved that the probe (i.e. PLL-GQDs) displayed penetration via endocytosis into the cell membrane. (B4) Overlay image confirmed the penetration of PLL-GQDs inside the nucleus of cells. Fig. 14 (C1 to C4) depicted the MTT assay. (C1) Illustrated a bright field image of HeLa cells without fabricated probe as the negative control, (C2) represented HeLa cells treated with 100 μg/mL of PLL-GQDs as the positive control, (C3) showed HeLa cells treated with Doxorubicin (STD) as the positive control, and (C4) described the percentage of viable HeLa cells after treatment with PLL-GQDs against the both negative and positive controls.

PLL-GQDs were shown to be extremely biocompatible with HeLa cells (Fig. 14-C4). Even at greater concentrations of up to 100 μg/mL, HeLa cells were found to be more than 84% alive. PLL-GQDs' superior biocompatibility makes them ideal candidates for the development of “smart biosensors” for intracellular applications. Overall, it committed that the synthesized probe (PLL-GQDs) was extremely biocompatible, with remarkable translocation properties in cell and nuclear membranes.

We demonstrated a facile design for synthesis of GQDs via hydrothermal method using a natural precursor (pistachio shells). Synthesized GQDs were surfaced functionalized using polycationic PLL to obtain a robust fluorescence turn “on-off” probe (PLL-GQDs) for highly selective sensing of cys and hcys. Surface functionalization of GQDs is perhaps due to attachment of cationic (-NH3 +) groups of PLL on the carboxyl (-COOH) and hydroxyl (-OH) groups rich surfaces of the GQDs. This binding of PLL cause the increase in PL intensity of sensor, with increase in PL decay time from 1.86 to 2.94 ns. The biomarkers cys and hcys specifically bind to the surfaces of PLL-GQDs via ionic and hydrophobic interactions resulting in fluorescence quenching. This linear relationship between the modulation in PL intensity of sensor upon addition of cys (0–150 nM) and hcys (0–100 nM) enable selective quantitative determination of cys and hcys against interfering molecules. The limit of detection for cys and hcys were found to be 2.38 and 1.94 nM, respectively in BPS (pH 7.4). Additionally, the probe was successfully used to detect the cys and hcys in human serum sample and the recoveries were 96.63–102.63%. The probe also showed the excellent cellular uptake remarkably for HeLa cells, enabling its future scope of sensing cys and hcys in cancer cells. On top of that, the MTT assay indicated that the method was biocompatible for recognition of cys and hcys in living cells or tissue.

Zamir G. Khan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Pravin O. Patil: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The Authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are highly obliged to the H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur, (India) for providing necessary facilities. We are grateful to Averin biotech private ltd, Hyderabad, Telangana, India for MTT assay and cell imaging characterization. We are thankful to IIT, Roorkee, IIT Madras, Chennai and STIC, Cochin India for providing XPS analysis facilities, time resolved emission experiments and other essential characterization facilities.
